risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Eligible participants were recruited from University of Cape Town (UCT) affiliated hospitals ... and were randomly allocated to one of the two treatment groups (active or placebo) according to a computer-generated randomization list. Randomization numbers were assigned consecutively." Comment: The study explicitly states that allocation used a computer-generated randomization list, which is an appropriate random method for sequence generation.

Quote: "Eligible participants were recruited ... and were randomly allocated to one of the two treatment groups (active or placebo) according to a computer-generated randomization list. Randomization numbers were assigned consecutively. Escitalopram (10 mg) and placebo were supplied as capsules of identical appearance. Medication was supplied in wallet cards that were dispensed at each visit. Wallet cards were identified by visit (i.e., visit 2, visit 3, etc.) with the escitalopram and placebo packed by the study pharmacist in sequentially numbered identical blister packs. Participants and investigators were blinded to treatment allocation..." Comment: The study explicitly describes a concealment mechanism consistent with sequentially numbered identical drug containers/blister packs prepared by the study pharmacist, which would prevent the person enrolling participants from foreseeing the next assignment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This was a prospective, randomized, double-blind placebo controlled trial..." and "Escitalopram (10 mg) and placebo were supplied as capsules of identical appearance... Participants and investigators were blinded to treatment allocation and there were no instances of un-blinding." Comment: The report explicitly states that both participants and investigators/personnel were blinded, placebo and active treatment were made identical in appearance, and no unblinding occurred. Although the study measured subjective outcomes (e.g. CAPS, CGI, VAS, SDS), the described blinding appears adequate and unlikely to have been broken, so performance bias is low.

Quote: "This was a prospective, randomized, double-blind placebo controlled trial... Participants and investigators were blinded to treatment allocation and there were no instances of un-blinding." Quote: "At each assessment, rating scales were administered..." Comment: Outcomes were assessed by clinicians/investigators for the primary and several secondary measures (e.g. CAPS, CGI, MADRS), and the study explicitly states that investigators were blinded to allocation with no unblinding. Although some outcomes were subjective and included patient self-report measures (VAS-D, VAS-A, SDS), participants were also blinded in this double-blind placebo-controlled trial, so knowledge of allocation was unlikely to influence outcome measurement.

Quote: "31 participants who met the following criteria were included in the study... Two participants (one from each treatment group) were excluded prior to the first post-baseline assessment and analysis as they were not contactable. The sample thus comprised of 12 participants on escitalopram and 17 on placebo..." and "Using an intent-to-treat sample, defined as all participants with at least one post-baseline CAPS assessment... The mixed model approach allows for patients with incomplete data to be included and utilizes the data that is available for all patients." and "Adherence to treatment was acceptable in both groups, with only 2 participants in each group withdrawn." Comment: Post-randomization losses were reported and were fairly balanced between arms: 1 per arm lost before first post-baseline assessment, plus 2 per arm withdrawn during treatment. This is approximately 6/31 total post-randomization attrition (~19%), without major imbalance between groups, and the primary CAPS outcome was analysed with an ITT-defined sample using mixed-model repeated measures that included participants with incomplete data. Although reasons for all later withdrawals are not fully detailed, the reported attrition is moderate and balanced rather than clearly biasing one arm.
